content.full_text:
Federal preemption 
2 U.S.C. 431(b) provides that a person is a candidate 
under the Federal Election Campaign Act if he seeks nomination or 
election to a federal elective office. 
The FEC has provided by 
regulation that a person is not a candidate if he does not actually 
seek nomination to an office. 
Thus, funds raised to "test the 
water" by a person who ultimately does not seek nomination to an 
office are not reportable. 
The question arose as to whether California was pre-
empted from requiring reporting of federal testing the water 
money. 
Nancy Simmons of the FEC in consultation with the general counsel 
advised me that California clearly could require that state campaign 
statements be amended to disclose federal testing the water money 
when the person did not become a federal candidate. 
If California 
wishes to require the federal testing the water money to be reported 
prior to the time that the person knows whether or not he will ulti-
mately be a federal candidate, she suggested that we write for a 
general counsel's opinion. 
She indicated that they might hold that 
California would not be preempted until the person in question actually 
becomes a federal candidate.

sections.question_synthetic: Is California preempted by federal law from requiring the disclosure of federal 'testing the water' funds raised by persons who do not ultimately become federal candidates?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: California can clearly require state campaign statements to be amended to disclose federal testing the water money when a person does not become a federal candidate. If California wishes to require reporting of such funds before the person decides whether to become a candidate, the FEC general counsel should be consulted, as they may hold that preemption does not apply until the person actually becomes a federal candidate.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks traditional QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers but contains analysis and conclusions. The document appears to be internal FPPC analysis rather than a formal response letter. No formal question statement is presented verbatim. Content discusses federal preemption and consultation with FEC.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is California preempted by federal law from requiring the disclosure of federal 'testing the water' funds raised by persons who do not ultimately become federal candidates?

CONCLUSION: California can clearly require state campaign statements to be amended to disclose federal testing the water money when a person does not become a federal candidate. If California wishes to require reporting of such funds before the person decides whether to become a candidate, the FEC general counsel should be consulted, as they may hold that preemption does not apply until the person actually becomes a federal candidate.
embedding.first_500_words: Federal preemption 2 U.S.C. 431(b) provides that a person is a candidate under the Federal Election Campaign Act if he seeks nomination or election to a federal elective office. The FEC has provided by regulation that a person is not a candidate if he does not actually seek nomination to an office. Thus, funds raised to "test the water" by a person who ultimately does not seek nomination to an office are not reportable. The question arose as to whether California was pre- empted from requiring reporting of federal testing the water money. Nancy Simmons of the FEC in consultation with the general counsel advised me that California clearly could require that state campaign statements be amended to disclose federal testing the water money when the person did not become a federal candidate. If California wishes to require the federal testing the water money to be reported prior to the time that the person knows whether or not he will ulti- mately be a federal candidate, she suggested that we write for a general counsel's opinion. She indicated that they might hold that California would not be preempted until the person in question actually becomes a federal candidate.
embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter addresses whether California is preempted from requiring disclosure of federal 'testing the water' campaign funds under the Federal Election Campaign Act, consulting with the FEC on the preemption issue.
